title: Hashmaps / Dictionaries
description: The most important data structure in coding interviews. Store key-value pairs for O(1) lookup.

sections.title: Creating a hashmap
sections.explanation: A hashmap stores key→value pairs. In interviews, you almost always use it to track seen values, count occurrences, or map one value to another.

snippets.python:
# Empty dict
seen = {}
seen = dict()

# Pre-filled
counts = {"a": 1, "b": 2}

# defaultdict — auto-initializes missing keys
from collections import defaultdict
freq = defaultdict(int)
freq["x"] += 1  # No KeyError, starts at 0

sections.title: Insert and check keys
sections.explanation: The core interview operation: check if a key exists, then insert or retrieve.

snippets.python:
# Insert
seen[key] = value

# Check existence
if key in seen:
    return seen[key]

# Get with default (avoids KeyError)
val = seen.get(key, 0)

# Delete
del seen[key]

sections.title: Looping over a hashmap
sections.explanation: Iterating keys, values, or both. Common in frequency counting and grouping problems.

sections.title: Frequency counting
sections.explanation: Counting occurrences is one of the most common hashmap patterns. Every language has a shortcut.

snippets.python:
from collections import Counter

# Count characters
freq = Counter("aabbc")
# Counter({'a': 2, 'b': 2, 'c': 1})

# Count list elements
freq = Counter([1, 2, 2, 3, 3, 3])

# Most common
freq.most_common(2)  # [(3, 3), (2, 2)]

sections.title: Interview Pattern: Complement Lookup
sections.explanation: The most common hashmap pattern: for each element, check if its complement exists. Used in Two Sum, pair sum problems, and target-based searches.

sections.title: Gotcha: Key types and mutability
sections.explanation: Not everything can be a hashmap key. Knowing the rules avoids subtle bugs.

snippets.python:
# ✅ Immutable types work as keys
seen = {(1, 2): True}   # tuple OK
seen = {"abc": True}     # string OK
seen = {42: True}        # int OK

# ❌ Mutable types fail
# seen = {[1, 2]: True}  # TypeError: unhashable type: 'list'

# Convert list to tuple for use as key
key = tuple(sorted(my_list))
